title: Spinning Portland breed fleece
subtitle: Working with the fine, short-staple fleece of a critically rare British heritage breed
excerpt: Portland fleece is fine (26 to 28 micron), short-staple (4 to 7 cm), and naturally pale cream. It cards into rolags for a woollen draw and suits medium-weight knitwear with a slightly rustic surface.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from the Rare Breeds Survival Trust breed descriptions, 'The Fleece and Fiber Sourcebook' (Robson and Ekarius), and general short-staple wool spinning practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A soft tube of carded fibre rolled off hand cards, used for a woollen draw. Produces a lofty, airy yarn with a halo.
A drafting technique in which the fibres are not aligned during spinning, allowing air to be trapped in the yarn. Produces a softer, loftier fabric than a worsted draw.
The natural waviness of a wool fibre. Finer wools have tighter, more regular crimp. Crimp gives wool its elasticity and felting ability.
The length of an individual lock of fleece, measured from cut end to tip before preparation.
One thousandth of a millimetre; the standard unit for fibre diameter. Lower micron count equals finer fibre.

content.text: Portland sheep are among the rarest breeds in Britain, listed as Priority by the Rare Breeds Survival Trust. Originally from the Isle of Portland in Dorset, they are a fine-medium primitive breed with a fleece that sits at 26 to 28
microns
in diameter and a
staple length
of 4 to 7 cm. The natural colour is off-white to pale cream. Even
crimp

structure and a modest micron count make the fleece well-suited to garment spinning, but the short staple means preparation and wheel setup both require attention. Portland fleece is not commercially available in combed top form; it arrives as a raw or lightly scoured fleece and must be prepared at home.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep hair and clothing back from the drive band, flyer, and bobbin. Tie hair back and roll sleeves before sitting at the wheel.

items.name: Portland breed fleece, scoured and dried

content.text: Method
Step 1: Scour and dry the fleece

Soak the locks in 55 to 60 °C water with a small amount of dish soap for 20 minutes without agitation. Lift out, rinse in water of the same temperature, and lay flat on a mesh screen to dry. Portland fleece has moderate lanolin; a single scour is usually sufficient. The locks should feel clean and slightly springy when fully dry.
Step 2: Open the locks and card into rolags

Tease each lock open by pulling the tip and cut ends apart gently. Feed small amounts onto the drum carder, perpendicular to the drum rotation, and card until the batt is even and cohesive. Lift off the batt and roll it into
rolags
lengthwise. The rolag preparation introduces air into the fibre structure and is the basis for the woollen draw.
Step 3: Set up the wheel

Choose a whorl that gives a medium-to-high twist ratio: around 10 to 12 twists per 30 cm of treadle is appropriate for the 4 to 7 cm staple. Set the drive-band tension to pull the yarn onto the bobbin steadily without dragging the fibre supply from the hand. A Saxony wheel with its larger drive wheel is well-suited to the sustained, rhythmic treadling that medium-weight woollen spinning requires.
Step 4: Spin with a woollen draw
Hold a rolag loosely in the back hand. With the forward hand pinching close to the orifice, draw the back hand away in a long, continuous motion. Use a
woollen draw

: release the pinch of the forward hand to allow the twist to travel back through the entire draft zone. The twist distributes along the attenuated fibre, trapping air and producing a lofty, soft yarn with a halo. Portland's even crimp makes it responsive to this method: the fibres interlock well even without full parallelisation.
Step 5: Ply and finish

Spin two bobbins of singles, then ply in the opposite direction. Portland takes dye readily because of its moderate crimp and clean scale structure; a 2-ply yarn dyed before or after plying suits both knit and woven cloth. Wind onto the niddy-noddy, tie, and soak at 40 °C to set the twist and bloom the yarn.

items.problem: Singles break frequently during the woollen draw
items.cause: Draw length exceeds the staple length before enough twist enters the draft zone
items.fix: Shorten the initial draw to 5 to 6 cm and allow twist to enter before extending further. Increase the whorl ratio slightly.

items.problem: Finished yarn is flat and dense rather than lofty
items.cause: Pinch hand held too long during the draw, compressing the fibres
items.fix: Release the forward-hand pinch earlier, when the draft zone is still extended, to allow the twist to distribute freely.

items.cause: Natural fleece has slight colour variation across the fleece. Uneven dye uptake is normal, not a preparation mistake.
items.fix: Scour the skein thoroughly before dyeing. A 10-minute pre-soak in warm water with a little white vinegar helps the dye enter the fibre evenly.
